title: AI Playbook - Coordinators
subtitle: Practical, role-aware guidance for Eduversal HQ Directors and Subject Specialists

audience._note: Directors hold cross-subject strategic authority and bypass subject filters; coordinators are subject specialists scoped to ch_subjects[]. The playbook serves both, with subject-specialist workflows being the bulk of the content. Directors also read leader-playbook.json for institutional-level work.

_meta.purpose: What an HQ Subject Specialist actually needs: practical guidance to use AI in curriculum authoring, assessment item drafting, and network-wide pedagogical work — without compromising the integrity of network-uniform assessment or Cambridge alignment.
_meta.referenceLayer: ../reference/ — verbatim Eduversal v1.0 + UNESCO + external sources.
_meta.doNotFabricate: Every workflow / pattern / red-flag carries a provenance object. Lint enforces non-empty provenance.
_meta.specialResponsibility: HQ Specialists author network-uniform content (chapter tests, EASE items, scheme of work, pacing). Errors here propagate to every Eduversal school. The bar for AI use in this work is higher than in any other role.

howToUseThisPlaybook.firstDay: Read 'orientation' + 'theAssessmentBoundary' + 'redFlags'. These three sections define what makes Specialist AI use different from Teacher AI use.
howToUseThisPlaybook.firstWeek: Pick ONE curriculum-authoring workflow from 'coreWorkflows' and trial it on low-stakes content (e.g. practice questions before chapter test items).
howToUseThisPlaybook.firstMonth: Establish your own AI-drafting + human-validation rhythm for the content you author most often. Document your prompts; you'll reuse them dozens of times across academic years.
howToUseThisPlaybook.ongoing: AI accelerates the volume of high-quality first drafts. Your value remains in subject judgement, Cambridge alignment, and item-level quality control.

orientation.what_this_is: HQ Subject Specialists author the content that propagates to every Eduversal school: chapter tests, EASE items, scheme of work, pacing, scheme-aligned resources. Your AI use must respect this leverage. A teacher's bad AI-assisted lesson plan affects one class. Your bad AI-assisted chapter test item affects every partner school's Year 8.
what_changed_for_specialists_in_2026: Cambridge International's Feb 2026 Primary + Lower Secondary Digital Literacy curriculum refresh requires alignment review of all CH-authored materials for those grades.
Eduversal's own item-bank refresh (in progress 2026) integrates Latihan.id imports, IGCSE Tools ExamView imports, and HQ-authored items — AI plays a role in cataloguing and quality-checking these.
Anthropic Claude for Education + ChatGPT for Teachers + Gemini for Education all offer school-tier products in 2025-2026. Specialists working with student data benefit from school-tier defaults.
Indonesia's Permendikdasmen 13/2025 (Koding & AI elective) creates new curriculum-authoring needs that may land on HQ Specialists if Eduversal adopts the elective network-wide.
orientation.the_specialist_question_to_keep_asking: If this content were published to every Eduversal school tomorrow and contained a subtle AI-introduced error, how would I know? Build review processes that answer that question concretely.

anchors: reference:eduversal-v1-part1-teacher.leader (Specialists operate at network leverage, not classroom leverage)

theAssessmentBoundary.title: The single most important rule for Specialists
theAssessmentBoundary.rule: AI may NEVER author final-form items for the formal assessment pipeline. AI may assist drafting; humans validate, edit, and approve.

formalAssessmentPipeline_aiAuthoringForb: Cambridge mock papers and centre-graded coursework drafts
Anything that writes to chapter_mastery or ease_growth Firestore collections
practicePipeline_aiAssistanceAllowed: practice_questions (self-study only, never feeds formal grading per Common Mistake #33)
practice_assessments (HQ-composed bundles for SH gamification, see Common Mistake #38)
Internal HQ working drafts that go through human review before becoming formal items

whyThisBoundaryExists: Network-uniform assessment is the system of record for Cambridge alignment and Eduversal's growth assessment claims. Item-level quality, distractor design, and cultural bias review are HQ subject-specialist responsibilities precisely because they require expert judgement that AI lacks.
AI-authored items leak factual errors, distractor flaws, and cultural biases that aren't caught without expert review.
The line between formal and practice pipelines is what protects formal assessment integrity. It cannot be allowed to blur.

whatYouCanDoWithAi.draftingStems: Yes — AI can draft candidate stems. You select, refine, validate, and finalise.
whatYouCanDoWithAi.draftingDistractors: Yes — AI can suggest distractors. You evaluate each for cognitive-demand validity, common-misconception alignment, and absence of cultural bias.
whatYouCanDoWithAi.draftingMarkScheme: Yes — AI can draft marking schemes. You moderate against department standards and Cambridge expectations.
whatYouCanDoWithAi.syllabusAlignmentCheck: Yes — AI can cross-reference items against a syllabus specification. You verify the cross-reference is accurate.
whatYouCanDoWithAi.reviewingFinishedItems: Yes — AI can spot certain quality issues (ambiguity, unclear language, broken numerical setups). You confirm.
whatYouCanDoWithAi.finalApproval: Never — final-form items going into chapter_test_items / ease_items require human author, human reviewer, and documented sign-off.

anchors: Eduversal monorepo Common Mistake #33 (practice_questions never feeds formal grading)
Eduversal monorepo Common Mistake #38 (practice_assessments never feeds chapter_mastery or ease_growth)
reference:eduversal-v1-part1-teacher.leader.domainC (Teachers lead development of student AI literacy curriculum, ensuring systematic and age-appropriate instruction)

curriculumAuthoring.title: Drafting scheme of work, pacing, and unit plans with AI
curriculumAuthoring.subjectScope: All HQ-authored curriculum content within your ch_subjects[]
curriculumAuthoring.philosophy: Scheme of work is the highest-leverage content you author. A scheme used network-wide for a year teaches thousands of student-hours. AI accelerates first-draft volume; your judgement on sequence, prerequisite mapping, and Cambridge alignment remains irreplaceable.

patterns.how: Prompt: 'You are drafting scheme of work for [Cambridge syllabus code + year]. Topic: [unit name]. Syllabus reference: [exact section]. Produce a [N]-lesson unit plan including learning objectives mapped to syllabus assessment objectives, prerequisite knowledge from prior units, common misconceptions, and suggested formative checks.' Then validate every syllabus AO mapping by hand.
patterns.why: AI often produces plausible-sounding AO mappings that don't match the actual Cambridge specification. The text reads well; the mapping is wrong. Manual verification is non-negotiable.

patterns.how: When updating Stage 7 → 8 → 9 progression grids (cambridge_syllabus_progression), prompt AI to identify dependencies between the stages you're working on. Then validate against your subject-specialist judgement of how students actually develop.
patterns.why: AI is good at surface dependency analysis (this concept presupposes that concept). It is weak at developmental dependency (students need this readiness before this concept lands). Use AI for the first; rely on yourself for the second.

patterns.how: When pacing across the Indonesian academic calendar (Jakarta-aligned, religious holidays, exam windows), give AI the calendar and ask for placement options. Specialist judgement decides which placement actually works.
patterns.why: Calendar mechanics are pattern-shaped. Pedagogical placement (does this concept land better before or after a holiday break?) is judgement.

patterns.how: After AI drafts a unit, run a second-pass prompt: 'Review this unit for Indonesia-specific contextualisation. Replace examples that assume Western/American context with Indonesian equivalents (currency, place names, food, climate, cultural references). Flag any examples that don't translate.'
patterns.why: Default AI output skews Western. Without explicit contextualisation, your scheme will feel imported. With one extra prompt pass, it feels local. Note: AI sometimes invents plausible-but-wrong Indonesian details — verify specifics.

antiPatterns: Don't accept AI's first AO-mapping suggestions without checking the Cambridge specification document directly.
Don't let AI invent Indonesian historical figures, place names, or cultural references — it confabulates plausible-sounding fakes.
Don't skip the misconception list. AI generates these reliably, and they're high-leverage for downstream teacher use.

toolsForThisWorkflow.primary: Claude for Education — strongest for long-document curriculum drafting where careful sequencing matters. Most cautious tone on cultural-bias and reduces fabrication of specifics.
toolsForThisWorkflow.secondary: ChatGPT for Teachers — faster iteration when you need many alternative drafts quickly (e.g. brainstorming starter activities, comparing scheme-of-work sequences).
toolsForThisWorkflow.forCrossReferencingMultipleDocs: NotebookLM — load Cambridge syllabus, prior year's scheme, examiner reports, your draft. Cross-document questions surface inconsistencies fast.
toolsForThisWorkflow.doNotUseForFinalAuthoring: Any AI — final curriculum content is human-authored. AI drafts; you author. (Same boundary as item drafting.)

itemDrafting.title: Drafting assessment items with AI (within the assessment boundary)
itemDrafting.subjectScope: Practice questions, internal working drafts, and AI-assisted brainstorming for formal items (which then require full human authoring)
itemDrafting.philosophy: AI is good at generating candidate items at volume. The bottleneck has always been HQ specialist time per item, and AI removes that bottleneck — but only on the drafting side, not the validation side. Net throughput rises because drafting becomes cheaper, not because validation becomes shorter.

patterns.how: Prompt: 'For [topic], the most common Year [N] misconception is [misconception]. Draft 5 MCQ stems where a student holding this misconception would select a specific wrong answer. For each stem, identify which distractor surfaces the misconception.' Review each draft for genuineness of the misconception trap.
patterns.why: Misconception-targeted MCQs are the highest-pedagogical-value items and the hardest to draft fast. AI removes the time barrier on initial drafting; your judgement validates whether the trap genuinely catches the misconception.

patterns.how: For existing items (yours or imported from Latihan.id / IGCSE Tools), prompt: 'Evaluate the distractors in this MCQ. For each, identify: is it plausible enough that a student holding a specific misconception would choose it? Or is it implausible (no student would reasonably select it)? Suggest improvements.' Review and apply selectively.
patterns.why: Imported item banks (especially auto-translated) often have weak distractors. AI distractor audit is fast and surfaces real issues.

patterns.how: Prompt: 'Review this item for cultural assumptions. Identify any references (names, places, scenarios, currency, food, customs) that assume Western/American context and would feel foreign to an Indonesian Year [N] student. Suggest substitutions.' This catches what default AI generation produces.
patterns.why: Cultural-bias review is high-value, low-effort with AI assistance. Without it, items feel imported.

patterns.how: For practice items (NOT for formal EASE items, which require Rasch-lite calibration), prompt: 'Estimate the difficulty of this item for Year [N]. Cite your reasoning.' Use as a sanity check, not as a calibration source.
patterns.why: AI difficulty estimates are unreliable but useful as a sanity check. For EASE items, the formal calibration process (calibrateEaseItems Cloud Function, MIN_CALIBRATION_RESPONSES = 30) is the only authority.

antiPatterns: Don't let AI write final-form chapter test items or EASE items. AI drafts; humans author finally.
Don't trust AI difficulty estimates as substitutes for actual response data. Use them as quick sanity checks only.
Don't accept AI distractors without checking that the 'wrong' answer is actually wrong. AI sometimes generates technically defensible distractors that complicate the item.
Don't paste student response data into a public AI tool when auditing items. Use anonymised performance summaries only.

anchors: Eduversal monorepo §19 EASE adaptive engine (calibration process)
reference:eduversal-v1-part1-teacher.leader.domainB (Teachers evaluate and recommend AI tools for school adoption)

toolsForThisWorkflow.primary: Claude for Education or ChatGPT for Teachers — best for nuanced Cambridge-aligned item drafting when prompted with the syllabus and command words. Outperforms dedicated quiz tools for Stage 7-9 Checkpoint-style stems.
toolsForThisWorkflow.forBulkPracticeBankSeeding: Wayground (formerly Quizizz) AI — fast bulk MCQ generation from documents/topics. Use only for practice-bank items, never for formal pipeline. Note the rebrand: Quizizz redirected to Wayground in 2025.
toolsForThisWorkflow.forBloomTaggedItems: Questgen — generates Bloom-level-tagged MCQ / true-false / short-answer from passages. Useful for item-bank seeding from textbook chapters; always specialist-review for distractor quality.
toolsForThisWorkflow.doNotUseFor: Formal pipeline items (chapter_test_items, ease_items). No tool — generalist or specialised — should produce the final form of these. AI drafts only.
toolsForThisWorkflow.criticalNote: These tools accelerate drafting. They do NOT reduce the time you spend on validation. Net throughput rises only when validation discipline stays intact.

syllabusAlignmentAndUpdate.title: Tracking Cambridge changes and propagating them to network materials
syllabusAlignmentAndUpdate.subjectScope: All HQ-authored materials for your subjects when Cambridge updates syllabus, AO weightings, or assessment formats
syllabusAlignmentAndUpdate.philosophy: Cambridge updates land asynchronously across subjects and bulletins. Specialists are the network's distribution layer — your timely propagation is what keeps every school's materials aligned. AI helps with the volume of cross-referencing.

patterns.how: When a Cambridge bulletin lands, paste it into AI with prompt: 'Identify changes to [my subject] [my Cambridge syllabus code]. For each change, list: scope of change, materials potentially affected, urgency.' Validate every claim against the original bulletin.
patterns.why: Bulletins are dense and lawyerly. AI parses them quickly. But AI also misses subtle changes — your read is the final word.

patterns.how: Provide AI with the relevant materials (scheme of work, pacing, items) and the new syllabus reference. Prompt: 'Identify any materials that no longer align with the updated syllabus. Specify which exact element is misaligned.' Verify each flagged item.
patterns.why: Volume scan is fast with AI. Specifics still require expert eyes.

patterns.how: When propagating curriculum updates to schools, frame the update in Cambridge's 'confident, responsible, reflective, innovative, engaged' learner attribute language. AI can help align your communication to this framing.
patterns.why: Cambridge inspection reports use these attributes. Materials and communications that match this framing read better in Cambridge contexts.

antiPatterns: Don't propagate AI-summarised Cambridge updates without reading the original bulletin yourself. AI summarisation drops nuance that matters.
Don't update materials based on draft Cambridge guidance. Only final, published Cambridge guidance is the authority.

toolsForThisWorkflow.primary: NotebookLM — best single tool for Cambridge update work. Load the new bulletin alongside the previous syllabus version and your current materials. Ask cross-document questions: 'What changed?' / 'Which of my materials are now misaligned?'
toolsForThisWorkflow.secondaryForDistribution: Claude for Education — drafts termly briefings to Subject Leaders that synthesise the Cambridge update for distribution. Always verify Claude's summary against the original bulletin before sending.
toolsForThisWorkflow.doNotUse: Free / personal AI accounts when handling Cambridge unpublished or under-embargo content. Use only school-tier / enterprise tools with proper DPAs.

supportingTeachersAndSchools.title: Specialist-to-teacher and specialist-to-school workflows
supportingTeachersAndSchools.subjectScope: Walkthrough visits, departmental coaching, network-level communications to schools
supportingTeachersAndSchools.philosophy: Specialists visit schools 1-2 times per year for appraisal-style work. Between visits, your written communication and material updates are how you support teachers. AI helps you produce that volume without sacrificing voice.

patterns.how: After a school visit, paste your raw walkthrough notes (anonymised — no teacher names if cross-school synthesis) and prompt for a structured summary: patterns observed, strengths to surface, recurring issues to address in network communication. Use as your draft for the formal report.
patterns.why: Raw notes are unstructured by design. AI synthesises faster than re-writing from scratch.

patterns.how: Your termly subject-leader briefing (for Subject Leaders across all partner schools) benefits from AI first draft. Prompt: 'Draft a termly briefing covering [topics]. Audience is experienced Subject Leaders. Tone: professional, action-oriented, light on jargon.' Edit thoroughly — Subject Leaders detect generic AI tone.
patterns.why: Volume + tone consistency is where AI helps. Specialist voice is what you add in edit.

patterns.how: When you have walkthrough notes or assessment results from multiple schools, prompt AI to identify patterns: 'What concerns appear across these notes? What strengths? What questions should I raise at the Directors' meeting?' Use as input to your own analysis, not as the analysis itself.
patterns.why: Pattern detection at volume is what AI is good at. Causal interpretation is your job.

antiPatterns: Don't paste school-identifying or teacher-identifying data into a public AI tool. Use only school-tier products with appropriate contracts.
Don't send AI-drafted termly communications without significant editing. Subject Leaders are your most discerning audience.
Don't let AI synthesis replace your own walkthrough judgement. The synthesis surfaces patterns; the judgement decides what they mean.

toolsForThisWorkflow.forWalkthroughNotesSynthesis: Claude for Education — strong at structuring raw notes into themed summaries. Always paste anonymised notes only (no school names, no teacher names).
toolsForThisWorkflow.forTermlyBriefingsToSubjectLeaders: ChatGPT for Teachers or Claude for Education — first-draft briefing content that you heavily edit. Subject Leaders are your most discerning audience; AI tone is detected within one paragraph.
toolsForThisWorkflow.forCrossSchoolPatternDetection: NotebookLM — load anonymised walkthrough syntheses from multiple schools, ask pattern-detection questions. The synthesis is a starting point for your analysis, not the analysis itself.
toolsForThisWorkflow.doNotUse: Otter.ai or Fireflies.ai for recording school visit conversations. Network-level recording creates a confidential-content audit trail you don't want and may breach UU PDP for the staff being recorded.

redFlags._note: Network-wide unconditional NO for HQ Subject Specialists. The bar is higher than for any other role because the leverage is greater.

items.rule: NEVER let AI author final-form items for chapter_test_items, ease_items, or any other formal-assessment pipeline. Drafting is allowed; final authoring is not.
items.why: Network-uniform assessment integrity depends on expert authoring. AI-authored items leak factual errors, distractor flaws, and cultural biases that aren't caught without expert review.
items.safeAlternative: AI assists drafting → you author the final form, document your authoring decisions, submit for moderation.

items.rule: NEVER paraphrase Cambridge verbatim text into seed scripts, framework JSONs, or canonical content using AI.
items.why: Eduversal Common Mistake #9: Cambridge / Permendiknas verbatim text comes from docs/research/, never inlined. Editing the verbatim inside derivative content makes audit-trail provenance false.
items.safeAlternative: Reference Cambridge sources via chip family. Read the original. Use AI to summarise for your own understanding, never for canonical content.

items.rule: NEVER accept AI-generated Indonesian historical figures, place names, scientific facts, or cultural details without verification.
items.why: AI confabulates plausible-sounding Indonesian specifics. Network materials with fabricated local content fail credibility immediately and damage Eduversal's standing.
items.safeAlternative: Verify every Indonesian-specific detail against authoritative sources. Use AI to suggest structure and English-language framing; verify Indonesian specifics manually.
items.provenance: manifest:knownGaps.indonesianClassroomCulturalBiasStudy + Eduversal monorepo §19 Latihan import dead-image lesson

items.rule: NEVER use AI to grade, score, or rank student work, even when reviewing items at scale.
items.why: AI scoring at scale propagates errors at scale. Specialist judgement is exactly what AI cannot replace here.
items.safeAlternative: Use AI to surface candidates for review. You score.

items.rule: NEVER push curriculum updates network-wide based on draft, leaked, or unofficial Cambridge guidance.
items.why: Curriculum updates carry the weight of Eduversal HQ authority. Propagating unofficial information causes school-level disruption and Cambridge-relationship damage.
items.safeAlternative: Wait for final published Cambridge guidance. Use AI for impact analysis on the final guidance, not on drafts.

items.rule: NEVER use personal-account AI for sustained HQ work involving student response data, school-identifying information, or unpublished curriculum drafts.
items.why: HQ work touches data with network-wide implications. Personal-tier accounts typically default to using data for model training. Unpublished curriculum drafts in a personal account become training data for the model.
items.safeAlternative: Use only Eduversal-approved school-tier products with appropriate data-handling contracts.

canAiTouchThisContent.summary: Three-question check before using AI on HQ content
questions: Will this content propagate to formal assessment pipelines (chapter_test_items / ease_items / Cambridge mock papers)? If yes, AI may draft but not author final form. If no, AI assistance is broader.
Does this content involve student response data or school-identifying information? If yes, school-tier AI only, with documented data-handling contract.

Does this content involve Cambridge verbatim text or other canonical-archive material? If yes, AI may summarise for your own understanding but not produce derivative canonical content (Common Mistake #9).

schoolReportsConflictingAiPracticeAcross.summary: When you visit a school and find AI practice varying widely across departments
branches: First: don't issue ad-hoc corrections. Document the variance.
Second: identify whether variance reflects subject-genuine difference (math vs. English may legitimately use AI differently) or unmanaged drift (no school policy, each teacher improvising).
Third: feed back to the Academic Coordinator with specifics. Variance management is their operational role; your role is to surface, not to fix in passing.
Fourth: if a clear breach of Eduversal AI Framework v1.0 is happening, raise it formally — but breaches not violations of subject-area variation.

cambridgeBulletinAffectsMultipleSubjects.summary: When a Cambridge update affects subjects outside your ch_subjects[]
branches: If the update is purely informational, forward to Directors for distribution.
If the update has cross-subject implications (e.g. interdisciplinary coursework guidance), coordinate with the affected Specialists before acting individually.
If the update concerns Cambridge-wide standards (learner attributes, assessment principles), it's a Directors-level coordination, not a single-Specialist action.

progressionPath._note: HQ Specialists are expected at Practitioner level minimum (per the framework's expectation that specialists model exemplary practice). Movement toward Leader level is encouraged but not mandatory.
year_1_realistic_target: Foundation+Practitioner AI use established across all your routine workflows.
Your prompt library documented for routine HQ tasks.
Assessment-boundary discipline ingrained: you can explain to a colleague exactly why AI may draft but never author final formal items.
Cambridge AI guidance monitoring habit in place.
year_2_realistic_target: Practitioner level across all five domains (Knowledge, Application, Pedagogical, Critical Evaluation, Professional Contribution).
Contribution to network-wide AI guidance for your subject specialty.
Mentoring at least one school-level Subject Leader through their Practitioner-level progression.
leader_targets: Leader-level contribution may include: shaping network-wide AI item-bank quality standards, drafting Eduversal AI Competency Framework updates, leading network professional development sessions.
Not all Specialists need to reach Leader level. It represents specialist contribution to the network's AI direction, not the default expectation.
progressionPath.selfAssessmentRef: Annual self-assessment writes to Firestore ai_competency_self_assessments. For Specialists, your line manager is typically your Director or central_admin.

pairedDocuments.teacherPlaybook: teacher-playbook.json — read this to understand what teachers in schools are working with; your guidance to them should fit their reality
pairedDocuments.leaderPlaybook: leader-playbook.json — read this for institutional-level work; Directors particularly need this
pairedDocuments.promptLibrary: prompt-library.json — specialist-relevant prompts (curriculum drafting, item drafting, distractor audit, cultural-bias audit, syllabus alignment)
pairedDocuments.decisionTrees: decision-trees.json — full flowcharts for the decisionAids summaries above

openQuestions._note: Questions this playbook intentionally does not answer.
items: Whether Eduversal will adopt the Permendikdasmen 13/2025 Koding & AI elective network-wide — open. If yes, Specialists may need to author or curate elective curriculum materials.
Whether Eduversal will produce its own Bahasa Indonesia LLM benchmarking for educational content reliability — open. Currently relies on external benchmarks (Artificial Analysis Multilingual Index).
AI integration in Cambridge inspection contexts — Cambridge International's own approach is evolving. Specialists track via the AI hub but no Specialist-specific Cambridge guidance exists.

How the Eduversal item bank's three-source structure (HQ-authored + Latihan.id imports + IGCSE Tools imports) interacts with AI drafting workflows for new items — partially documented in monorepo Common Mistakes #31-36; specialist-specific patterns still emerging.
